tagline: Film room intelligence at machine speed.

round_of_64.reasoning: Duke's 39-point AdjEM gap is the second-largest in the bracket. Caleb Foster's foot fracture matters long-term but not here — Siena's -1.7 AdjEM means Duke wins comfortably with their backup rotation. Scheyer's 8-3 tournament record and ACC championship momentum seal it.

Ohio State's 8.4-point AdjEM edge is real, but Brandon Noel has been out since January and Chatman is day-to-day with a groin issue. TCU's Jamie Dixon has 14-15 career tournament wins across 16 appearances — massive coaching edge. TCU's hot 8-2 L10 stretch and Dixon's March pedigree flip this coin-flip matchup.

Rick Pitino's 55-22 tournament record is the best in the field. St. John's elite defense (AdjD 94.1) will suffocate Northern Iowa's slower offense, and the 14.6-point AdjEM gap is too wide for a classic 5-12 upset. Big East champions riding a 9-1 L10 wave with the best tournament coach alive.

Bill Self's 57-24 tournament record and 2 national titles make this a coaching mismatch of the highest order against Cal Baptist's first-ever tournament coach. Kansas's 16.3-point AdjEM gap is comfortable. Darryn Peterson's absence weakens Kansas's ceiling, but Cal Baptist's WAC-level defense (AdjD 100.3) can't contain the Jayhawks.

Louisville holds a 10.5-point AdjEM edge, but this is closer than it looks. Mikel Brown Jr.'s lower back injury is significant, and South Florida is on a scorching 9-1 L10 run as AAC champions with a 9-game win streak. Louisville's offensive firepower (AdjO 124.0) and tempo edge prevail, but barely — USF's momentum is real.

Tom Izzo's 59-26 tournament record with 8 Final Fours makes this automatic at the 3-14 line. Michigan State's 21.3-point AdjEM advantage is dominant. Divine Ugochukwu's season-ending loss hurts depth, but NDSU's AdjEM of 5.4 is simply not competitive. Izzo doesn't lose these.

UCLA's 8.5-point AdjEM advantage evaporates when you account for Tyler Bilodeau (leading scorer, out/limited) and Donovan Dent (calf, limited). That's their two best players compromised. UCF has moderate injuries too (Stillwell ankle, Bol chest), but UCLA's injury situation is more devastating. UCF's Big 12-tested roster exploits a depleted Bruins squad.

Dan Hurley's 15-5 tournament record with back-to-back national titles is the gold standard. UConn's 30.2-point AdjEM gap is massive. Jaylin Stewart's absence (missed Big East tournament) is a concern but irrelevant against a 15-seed. Furman's SoCon-level defense can't slow UConn's championship-caliber attack.

Florida's 43.8-point AdjEM gap is the largest in the bracket. Todd Golden's 6-2 tournament record and SEC-caliber roster make this an annihilation-level mismatch. Handlogten's medical redshirt doesn't matter when PVAMU's AdjD is 111.4. Florida's elite two-way profile (AdjO 126.1, AdjD 92.3) ensures a blowout from tip.

Iowa actually has the higher AdjEM (21.9 vs 19.6), and Clemson is devastated by injuries — Carter Welling (torn ACL, out) and Dillon Hunter (broken hand, out). That's two key contributors gone from the ACC tournament. Despite Iowa's cold 4-6 L10 stretch, Ben McCollum inherits a healthier roster that can exploit Clemson's depleted depth.

Vanderbilt's 17.1-point AdjEM gap and explosive offense (AdjO 127.4) are decisive. McNeese is 10-0 in their L10 as Southland champs, which demands respect, but the conference strength gap (SEC vs Southland) is enormous. Vanderbilt's SEC runner-up pedigree and offensive firepower are too much for McNeese's defense (AdjD 102.2).

Nebraska's 22.1-point AdjEM advantage and elite defense (AdjD 93.5) make this straightforward. Rienk Mast's illness (hospitalized before Big Ten tourney) is a minor concern, but Fred Hoiberg's squad has too much depth. Troy's Sun Belt offense (AdjO 110.7) will be strangled by Nebraska's defensive pressure.

UNC lost Caleb Wilson to a broken thumb — their leading scorer and projected top-5 pick. That transforms this matchup. VCU is 9-1 L10, A-10 champions running HAVOC defense with extraordinary momentum. The 5.2-point AdjEM gap shrinks dramatically without Wilson's production. VCU's defensive intensity and hot streak overwhelm a compromised Tar Heels squad playing their worst basketball (5-5 L10).

Illinois' AdjEM of 33.7 creates a 32.5-point gap that is insurmountable. The Illini have the second-best offense in the tournament (AdjO 131.9). Penn's Ethan Roberts is dealing with a concussion, further weakening an already overmatched squad. Brad Underwood's experience (8-9 tournament) vs Fran McCaffery's first year at Penn — no contest.

Saint Mary's holds a 3.3-point AdjEM edge with Randy Bennett's 7-11 tournament record and 25 years of program-building continuity. Texas A&M's Bucky McMillan is in his first year and has just 0-1 tournament experience. Saint Mary's 8-2 L10 hot streak and Bennett's half-court defensive system will slow A&M's tempo and grind out a win. The coaching mismatch favors the Gaels.

Kelvin Sampson's 31-21 tournament record with 4 Final Fours is elite. Houston's 30.4-point AdjEM gap and nation-leading defense (AdjD 92.4) will suffocate Idaho's modest offense. The 8-2 L10 HOT momentum and Big 12 pedigree make this a comfortable cruise against a first-time tournament coach.

Arizona's 41.6-point AdjEM gap and Big 12 championship momentum (9-1 L10) make this the second-biggest mismatch in the bracket. Jaden Bradley's taped wrist is minor and won't matter against a -6.1 AdjEM team. Tommy Lloyd's squad demolishes LIU's first-time tournament coach and NEC-level roster.

Utah State's AdjEM (20.9) actually exceeds Villanova's (19.1), and they're riding a 9-1 L10 streak as MWC double champions (regular season + tournament). Kevin Willard's 4-7 tournament record in his first year at Nova doesn't inspire confidence. Utah State's offensive efficiency (AdjO 123.0) and HOT momentum overwhelm a lukewarm Villanova squad.

Wisconsin's 17.3-point AdjEM advantage and Greg Gard's 7-7 tournament experience are sufficient. High Point is 9-1 L10 with a 30-4 record as Big South champs, which demands respect in classic 5-12 territory, but the conference strength gap is massive. Wisconsin's Big Ten physicality and offensive firepower (AdjO 127.2) are too much.

John Calipari's 57-22 tournament record (6 FFs, 1 title) is among the best ever at the 4-13 line. Arkansas's 21.3-point AdjEM gap, SEC championship momentum (8-2 L10), and explosive offense (AdjO 127.9) overwhelm Hawaii. Darius Acuff Jr. returned for the SEC tourney, and Hawaii's cross-Pacific travel adds fatigue.

BYU's 2.7-point AdjEM edge is slim but real, with slightly better offense and defense. Texas didn't even qualify for the SEC tournament (5-5 L10), signaling poor late-season form. Kevin Young's 2-1 tournament record in just 2 apps is promising against Sean Miller's first year at Texas. BYU's marginal efficiency edges hold in a tight 6-11 game.

Gonzaga's 25-point AdjEM gap and Mark Few's 44-25 tournament record are commanding. Braden Huff's dislocated kneecap (out until Sweet 16 at earliest) and Jalen Warley's thigh contusion are notable losses, but the 3-14 seed gap is too wide for a first-time tournament coach from CUSA. Gonzaga's 9-1 L10 WCC championship momentum carries them, though Huff's absence lowers the ceiling.

Miami's 2.7-point AdjEM edge is modest, but their 8-2 L10 HOT momentum vs Missouri's 6-4 neutral form is the separator. Jai Lucas is a first-year tournament coach, but so is Dennis Gates functionally (1-3 record). Miami's ACC-tested defense and momentum carry this tight 7-10 matchup.

Purdue's nation-best offense (AdjO 133.3) meets Queens' worst defense in the bracket (AdjD 117.1). The 35.5-point AdjEM gap, Big Ten championship momentum (8-2 L10), and Matt Painter's 22-16 tournament record make this a showcase game. Queens is in their first-ever D1 tournament with a first-year tournament coach.

Michigan's 38.3-point AdjEM gap and second-best defense in the country (AdjD 91.0) make this a blowout. Dusty May's 6-3 tournament record (including a Final Four) and 8-2 L10 momentum are commanding. Howard's limited tournament history and 109.2 AdjD will be overwhelmed by Michigan's fast pace (71.2).

The 2.3-point AdjEM gap slightly favors Georgia, but Saint Louis is 8-2 L10 HOT with a 28-5 record — they're peaking at the right time. Josh Schertz's A-10 program has quietly built an elite season. Georgia at 6-4 L10 is the definition of neutral momentum. In a true coin-flip 8-9 game, the team playing better basketball right now wins, and that's Saint Louis.

Texas Tech's 15.7-point AdjEM advantage and Big 12-caliber defense (AdjD 98.8) handle Akron's MAC offense comfortably. Grant McCasland's 3-3 tournament record against John Groce's 8-8 is a wash, but the conference strength gap and Texas Tech's offensive firepower (AdjO 126.3) seal it.

Nate Oats' 11-6 tournament record and Alabama's explosive offense (AdjO 129.5) at the fastest tempo in the bracket (73.1) overwhelm Hofstra's first-ever tournament coach. The 18.5-point AdjEM gap and SEC physicality make this comfortable. Hofstra's CAA defense (AdjD 105.0) can't slow Alabama's transition game.

Tennessee's elite defense (AdjD 95.5) and 7.9-point AdjEM advantage are decisive. Rick Barnes' 28-26 tournament record across 21 appearances gives massive experience edge over Andy Enfield's 5-5 record. Nate Ament's knee/ankle return for the SEC tourney is a positive sign. Tennessee's defensive identity suffocates Miami Ohio's mid-range game.

Virginia's 23-point AdjEM gap and 8-2 L10 HOT momentum as ACC runner-up are commanding. Ron Sanchez is a first-year tournament coach, which introduces some uncertainty, but the talent and system gap against Wright State's Horizon League roster is enormous. Virginia's packline defense (AdjD 95.8) will grind Wright State's offense to a halt.

Santa Clara's AdjEM (21.3) actually exceeds Kentucky's (19.9) even before accounting for Kentucky's devastating injury report: Quaintance OUT, Lowe OUT for season, Moreno questionable. That's 3 of their best players compromised. Mark Pope's 2-3 tournament record offers no safety net. Santa Clara is healthy, 7-3 L10 from the WCC, and the numbers flatly favor them. This isn't a gut-feel upset — it's what the data demands.

Iowa State's 35.2-point AdjEM gap and elite defense (AdjD 92.6) make this automatic. T.J. Otzelberger's 9-5 tournament record against Tennessee State's first-ever tournament coach is a massive experience gap. The Cyclones' defensive system suffocates 15-seeds.

round_of_32.reasoning: Duke's AdjEM of 37.3 towers over TCU's 15.2 — a 22.1-point gap. Even with Caleb Foster's foot fracture, Duke's offensive depth (AdjO 128.1) and elite defense (AdjD 90.8) are overwhelming. Jamie Dixon's tournament pedigree can't overcome a talent gap this wide. Scheyer's ACC championship squad rolls.

St. John's AdjEM (25.7) actually exceeds Kansas's (23.5), and Rick Pitino's 55-22 tournament record rivals even Bill Self's 57-24. Kansas is weakened by Darryn Peterson's absence (missed 11 games) and a mediocre 5-5 L10 stretch. St. John's is 9-1 L10 as Big East champions with elite defense (AdjD 94.1). The hot team with the marginally better numbers gets the nod.

Michigan State's AdjEM (26.7) edges Louisville's (25.9), but the real separator is Tom Izzo's 59-26 tournament record vs Pat Kelsey's 0-6. Louisville's Mikel Brown Jr. back injury further tips the scales. Izzo's between-round adjustments and Michigan State's defensive discipline (AdjD 96.2) neutralize Louisville's offensive tempo.

UConn's AdjEM (28.1) is 13.8 points above UCF's (14.3). Even after UCF's R64 upset of depleted UCLA, UCF's modest defensive profile (AdjD 105.7) cannot contain Dan Hurley's two-time defending champions. UConn's championship DNA and efficiency dominance overwhelm UCF's Big 12 competitiveness.

Florida's AdjEM of 33.8 towers over Iowa's 21.9 — an 11.9-point gap. Florida's defense (AdjD 92.3) will suffocate Iowa's offense, and Florida's own offense (AdjO 126.1) overwhelms Iowa's weaker defense (AdjD 100.2). Todd Golden's 6-2 tournament record and SEC pedigree make this comfortable.

Vanderbilt's AdjEM (28.1) edges Nebraska's (23.8) by 4.3 points, driven by a massive offensive gap (AdjO 127.4 vs 117.3). Nebraska's elite defense (AdjD 93.5) is formidable, but Vanderbilt's SEC pedigree and explosive scoring overcome defensive resistance. Mast's illness adds uncertainty for Nebraska.

Illinois' AdjEM of 33.7 is 17.5 points above VCU's 16.2. VCU's HAVOC defense and upset momentum from beating UNC are real, but Illinois has the tournament's second-best offense (AdjO 131.9). Brad Underwood's experience neutralizes VCU's energy, and the efficiency gap is simply too wide for a mid-major to overcome two rounds in a row.

Houston's AdjEM (33.0) is 11.2 points above Saint Mary's (21.8). Both teams play slow, grinding half-court basketball, but Houston's defense (AdjD 92.4) is dramatically better than Saint Mary's (AdjD 97.7). Kelvin Sampson's 31-21 tournament record vs Bennett's 7-11 gives Houston the coaching edge in a style matchup that favors the Cougars' defensive identity.

Arizona's AdjEM (35.5) is 14.6 points above Utah State's (20.9). The Wildcats' elite defense (AdjD 91.4) will contain Utah State's strong offense (AdjO 123.0). Arizona's Big 12 championship momentum and Tommy Lloyd's 6-4 tournament record dominate Utah State's upset bid.

Arkansas's AdjEM (26.3) edges Wisconsin's (25.5), but the coaching gap is enormous: Calipari's 57-22 tournament record (6 FFs, 1 title) vs Gard's 7-7. Arkansas's SEC championship momentum (8-2 L10) and explosive offense (AdjO 127.9) at a fast tempo overwhelm Wisconsin's slower pace preference.

Gonzaga's AdjEM (26.3) holds a 5.9-point edge over BYU's (20.4) with dramatically better defense (AdjD 94.0 vs 104.3). Even without Braden Huff, Mark Few's 44-25 tournament record and WCC rivalry familiarity give Gonzaga the schematic edge. Few knows BYU's tendencies cold.

Purdue's AdjEM of 33.0 is 13.4 points above Miami's 19.6. Purdue's nation-best offense (AdjO 133.3) exploits Miami's average defense (AdjD 101.5). Matt Painter's 22-16 tournament record and Big Ten championship momentum make this straightforward against a first-year tournament coach.

Michigan's AdjEM (36.6) is 19.3 points above Saint Louis's (17.3). The Wolverines' elite defense (AdjD 91.0) will smother Saint Louis's offense (AdjO 119.6). Saint Louis's 28-5 record and hot streak earned them this spot, but the talent and efficiency gap against a #1 overall seed contender is too vast.

Alabama's AdjEM (26.7) barely trails Texas Tech's (27.5), but Alabama's explosive offense (AdjO 129.5) at a blazing tempo (73.1) creates a track meet. Nate Oats' 11-6 tournament record and SEC experience edge Grant McCasland's 3-3 Big 12 record. Alabama's pace and firepower create separation.

Virginia's AdjEM (26.4) edges Tennessee's (26.0) — razor-thin. Both are elite defensive teams at deliberate tempos. Virginia's 8-2 L10 HOT momentum as ACC runner-up vs Tennessee's 6-4 neutral stretch is the tiebreaker. In a low-possession grinder, the team playing better recent basketball gets the edge.

Iowa State's AdjEM (31.1) is 9.8 points above Santa Clara's (21.3). The Cyclones' elite defense (AdjD 92.6) will disrupt Santa Clara's offense. T.J. Otzelberger's 9-5 tournament record and Big 12 pedigree are too much for Santa Clara's WCC roster to overcome in the second round.

sweet_16.reasoning: Duke's AdjEM (37.3) leads St. John's (25.7) by 11.6 points. St. John's elite defense (AdjD 94.1) is the best Duke has faced, and Pitino's tactical genius will make this competitive, but Duke's generational offense (AdjO 128.1) creates too much scoring volume. Even with Foster out, Duke's depth and Scheyer's ACC title momentum carry them.

UConn's AdjEM (28.1) edges Michigan State's (26.7) by 1.4 points. Both are elite defensive teams, making this a half-court slugfest. Dan Hurley's 15-5 tournament record with 2 titles and UConn's late-game execution in March edge Izzo's experience. UConn's championship pedigree is the separator in a game that comes down to final possessions.

Florida's AdjEM (33.8) holds a 5.7-point edge over Vanderbilt's (28.1). Both are SEC teams with explosive offenses, but Florida's defense (AdjD 92.3 vs 99.3) is the clear separator — 7 points better. In an SEC rivalry Sweet 16, the team that gets stops advances, and Florida's two-way profile is significantly superior.

Houston's AdjEM (33.0) trails Illinois's (33.7) by 0.7 — essentially identical elite teams. But Houston's historically elite defense (AdjD 92.4) is built to slow Illinois' high-volume offense (AdjO 131.9). Kelvin Sampson's 31-21 tournament record with 4 Final Fours provides the March experience edge. Houston's defensive identity is specifically designed for opponents like Illinois.

Arizona's AdjEM (35.5) is 9.2 points above Arkansas's (26.3). Both play at fast tempos, creating a high-possession shootout — but Arizona is more efficient on both ends. Arizona's defense (AdjD 91.4 vs 101.7) is the decisive edge. Calipari's tournament pedigree keeps it competitive, but Tommy Lloyd's Big 12 champion is the better team.

Purdue's AdjEM (33.0) holds a 6.7-point edge over Gonzaga's (26.3). Purdue's nation-best offense (AdjO 133.3) overwhelms even Gonzaga's solid defense (AdjD 94.0). With Braden Huff potentially returning but at limited capacity, Gonzaga's depth is still compromised. Matt Painter's Big Ten champions and offensive firepower advance.

Michigan's AdjEM (36.6) is 9.9 points above Alabama's (26.7). Michigan's defense (AdjD 91.0) is built to slow Alabama's blazing tempo and force half-court execution. Alabama's explosive offense (AdjO 129.5) keeps it competitive, but Michigan's complete two-way profile is too balanced to overcome.

Iowa State's AdjEM (31.1) edges Virginia's (26.4) by 4.7 points. Both are elite defensive teams at slow tempos, but Iowa State's offensive efficiency (AdjO 123.8 vs 122.2) creates the margin. Otzelberger's 9-5 tournament record and Big 12 pedigree edge Ron Sanchez's first tournament appearance as HC.

elite_8.reasoning: Duke's AdjEM (37.3) holds a 9.2-point edge over UConn's (28.1). Duke's offense (AdjO 128.1) is the best in the field, and their defense (AdjD 90.8) is even better than UConn's (95.0). Caleb Foster might return for the Elite Eight, which adds a wild card. In a blue-blood Elite Eight matchup, Duke's complete statistical superiority on both ends is the difference.

Florida (AdjEM 33.8) vs Houston (33.0) — two elite teams separated by 0.8 points. Houston's defense is marginally better, but Florida's faster tempo (70.4 vs 63.3) creates more possessions where their offensive edge compounds. Florida's pace forces Houston out of their slow-grind comfort zone, and Todd Golden's 6-2 tournament record provides the experience edge.

Arizona's AdjEM (35.5) edges Purdue's (33.0) by 2.5 points. Purdue has the better offense (AdjO 133.3 vs 126.9), but Arizona's defense (AdjD 91.4 vs 100.3) is dramatically superior — nearly 9 points better. In an Elite Eight game, defense wins. Arizona's Big 12 championship pedigree and defensive elite status carry them.

Michigan's AdjEM (36.6) holds a 5.5-point edge over Iowa State's (31.1). Both have elite defenses (AdjD 91.0 vs 92.6), but Michigan's offense (AdjO 127.6 vs 123.8) is the separator. Dusty May's 6-3 tournament record (including an FF) gives Michigan the coaching edge. The Wolverines' complete profile advances to the Final Four.

final_four.reasoning: Duke's AdjEM (37.3) holds a 3.5-point edge over Florida's (33.8). Duke's offense (AdjO 128.1) is the best in the country and will exploit Florida's defense (AdjD 92.3), while Duke's defense (AdjD 90.8) is actually tighter than Florida's. Caleb Foster could be back by the Final Four, adding another weapon. Duke's complete two-way dominance is the separator against an excellent Florida team.

Michigan's AdjEM (36.6) edges Arizona's (35.5) by 1.1 points — razor-thin but consistent. Michigan's defense (AdjD 91.0) is marginally tighter than Arizona's (91.4), and their offense (AdjO 127.6 vs 126.9) holds a similar slim edge. Both play at fast tempos, creating a high-possession shootout where Michigan's fractional efficiency advantage accumulates over 40 minutes.

championship.reasoning: Duke's AdjEM (37.3) holds a 0.7-point edge over Michigan's (36.6). Duke's offense (AdjO 128.1 vs 127.6) and defense (AdjD 90.8 vs 91.0) are both marginally better. In a championship game between the top two seeds, Jon Scheyer's ACC title momentum and the potential return of Caleb Foster give Duke the thinnest of edges in what projects as the most competitive championship game in years.
